goal: Reach B2 in Icelandic and take the certification on 2026-09-15.
constraints: Weekday practice fixed: exactly 25 minutes each morning starting at 6:15.
Audio-only mode allowed during the 25-minute practice slot; no reading or writing.
Budget-friendly resources: avoid expensive weekly subscriptions; willing to pay for high-value one-off lessons or resources.
open: User preference for delivery: minute-by-minute routine for the 25 minutes or quick tips depending on goals
Preference: integrate small daily wins and streak tracking to support routine.

Mental habits for reducing overwhelm when learning declensions: role-first analysis, prioritize high-frequency cases, treat endings as chunks, pair cases with verbs/prepositions, use fixed frames, parse with a 3-step checklist, listen for case markers.
Strategy for vowel harmony: organic exposure vs targeted drills

Dialect variation vs standard variety: guidance on prioritization when choosing what to learn first; align with certification goals; start with standard, gradually add receptive exposure to dialects; consider local integration goals.
Practical: how to use corpora or frequency lists without getting overwhelmed by raw data
Prosody/intonation: evaluate transfer across language families and determine language-specific practice requirements

Loanword workflow for SRS: Spot lookalikes, verify meaning in a dictionary or corpus, create a natural example sentence, tag card as 'loan' or 'false friend' and add audio/production practice, review aggressively for the first weeks.
False friend patterns to watch for when learning Icelandic (cognates with shifted meaning, phonological/orthographic shifts, collocational and register differences); verify meaning and usage.
Icelandic loanword note: verify sense and usage for English lookalikes; Icelandic tends to coin native equivalents; do not assume lookalikes carry same meaning.
Prioritize frequency: rely more on high-frequency, transparent loans; be cautious with low-frequency lookalikes that are more likely to be misleading.
Get occasional native feedback: a quick correction from a speaker or tutor catches subtle meaning or register differences faster than guessing.

Idioms learning: treat as productive frames with slots; use an audio-first routine (2-4 idioms per week); collect 2-3 authentic audio examples per idiom; practice shadowing and substitution drills; produce a 30-60s monologue using the idioms; track prosody and register; seek occasional native feedback.
